videoTitle: Lesson 2.6- Creating a Marketing Automation Strategy Document (Template).mp4
transcript: Welcome back to the final lesson of Module 2. Over the past five lessons, you have learned some of the most critical skills for any marketing automation specialist. You learned how to conduct discovery calls that uncover the real needs and goals of your clients. You learned how to identify and document their products, services, and offers. You learned how to map complete customer journeys from the first moment of awareness all the way through to advocacy. You created detailed customer personas that bring your target audience to life. And you learned how to define business goals and translate them into specific, measurable KPIs. Now it is time to bring all of that work together into one comprehensive, professional document. The Marketing Automation Strategy Document. This document is going to become one of your most valuable deliverables as a marketing automation specialist. It is the blueprint that guides everything you build. It is the document that gets client buy-in before you start doing the work. And it is proof of your strategic thinking and professionalism. Here is why this matters so much, especially if you are just starting out. When you can present a well-crafted strategy document to a potential client, you immediately separate yourself from everyone else they might be talking to. Most people who claim to do marketing automation are really just technicians. They can push buttons and set up email sequences, but they cannot think strategically. They cannot articulate why they are doing what they are doing or how it connects the business out. You are going to be different. You are going to walk into client conversations with a clear plan, backed by research, tied to measurable goals, and present it in a professional document that shows you know exactly what you are doing. That is what gets you hired. That is what commands premium rates. And that is what turns one-time projects into long-term retainer relationships. In this lesson, I am going to walk you through exactly how to create a marketing automation strategy document from start to finish. You will learn what sections to include, what information goes in each section, how to present data and insights visually, and how to structure the document so it is easy to read and compelling to your client. By the end of this lesson, you will have a complete framework you can use for every client engagement. Let's dive in. Before we get into the structure of the document, let's talk about why creating a strategy document is so important, especially when you are new to marketing automation. First, a strategy document gets client buy-in and alignment before you start building anything. This is huge. One of the biggest mistakes new marketing automation specialists make is jumping straight into tactics without getting clear agreement on strategy. They start building landing pages or setting up email sequences without a documented plan, and then halfway through the project, the client says, wait, I thought we were doing something different, or I didn't realize this is what you meant. That leads to frustration, scope creep, and conflicts about payment. When you have a strategy document that the client reviews, approves, and signs off on before you start the work, everyone is on the same page. The client knows exactly what you are going to build, why you are building it, how long it will take, and what it will cost. That clarity protects both of you. Second, a strategy document provides a clear project roadmap. It breaks down a complex engagement into manageable phases with specific deliverables and timelines. This makes the project feel less overwhelming for the client and gives you a clear plan to follow. You know exactly what you need to build and in what order. Third, and this is really important, a strategy document protects you from scope creep. Scope creep is when a client keeps asking for more and more things that were not part of the original agreement. Can you also build this page? Can you also write these emails? Can you also set up this automation? Without a documented strategy, it is hard to push back because you never clearly defined what was in scope and what was out of scope. With a strategy document, you can say that is a great idea, but it is outside the scope of what we agreed to in the strategy document. We can absolutely add it, but it will require additional time and budget. Let's discuss. Fourth, a strategy document aligns all stakeholders. In many client engagements, especially with larger businesses, you are not just working with one person. There might be a business owner, a marketing manager, a sales director, and other team members involved. When everyone has access to the same strategy document, everyone understands the plan and their role in it. This reduces miscommunication and ensures smooth execution. Fifth, a strategy document demonstrates your professionalism. It shows that you are not just a freelancer or technician, but a strategic partner who thinks deeply about the client's business. Clients are willing to pay more for specialists who can think strategically and present their ideas professionally. Finally, a strategy document keeps everyone focused on goals. It is easy to get distracted by shiny objects or new ideas during a project. The strategy document acts as a north star that keeps everyone focused on the objectives you set at the beginning. Whenever someone suggests a new tactic or approach, you can evaluate it against the goals in the strategy document and decide whether it supports the plan or is a distraction. Now let's talk about what actually goes into a strategy document. Before we get into the structure of the document, let's talk about why creating a strategy document is so important, especially when you are new to marketing automation. A comprehensive marketing automation strategy document typically includes nine core sections. Now, do not let that intimidate you. You are going to build this one section at a time, and most of the content you need has already been created in your previous lessons. You are really just organizing and presenting information you have already gathered. Let me give you a quick overview of the nine sections and then we will go through each one in detail. Section 1 is the Executive Summary. This is a one-page overview of the entire strategy. it is designed for busy decision makers who might not read the full document. Even though it comes first in the document, you will actually write it last after you have completed all the other sections. Section 2 is Business Overview and Goals. This is where you document your understanding of the client's business, their current situation, and their primary goals and objectives. This comes directly from your discovery call in Lesson 2.1 and your goals work in Lesson 2.5. Section 3 is Target Audience and Customer Personas. Here you present the detailed personas you created in Lesson 2.4. This section should be highly visual with persona cards that include photos, demographics, goals, challenges, and preferred channels. Section 4 is Current State Analysis. This is where you document the client's starting point, their current marketing efforts, existing tools and platforms, baseline performance metrics, and gaps or opportunities you have identified. This establishes the before picture so you can measure the after results. Section 5 is Marketing Automation Strategy. This is the heart of the document where you present your recommended approach, you will outline the channels you will use, the types of campaigns you will run, the funnel design, the automation workflows, and the content strategy. Section 6 is implementation plan and timeline. This breaks down the project into phases with specific tasks, deliverables, responsibilities, and deadlines. This answers the question, how and when will everything get done? Section 7 is budget and resources. This is a transparent breakdown of all costs including your fees, platform subscriptions, add spend, and any other expenses. Transparency builds trust. Section 8 is Success Metrics and KPIs. Here you define exactly how success will be measured with specific KPIs, targets, and reporting cadence. This comes from your work in Lesson 2.5. Section 9 is Next Steps and Call to Action. This is where you outline what happens next, how the client can approve the strategy, what they need to provide, and when you can get started. Most strategy documents end up being between 15 and 25 pages depending on the complexity of the engagement. That might sound like a lot, but remember much of it is visual, persona cards, funnel diagrams, timelines, and tables. It is not just walls of text. Now let's go through each section in detail so you know exactly what to include. Let's start with Section 1. The Executive Summary. Even though this appears first in your document, I recommend writing it last after you have completed all the other sections. Why? Because the Executive Summary is a distillation of the entire strategy, and you cannot effectively summarize something you have not written yet. The Executive Summary should be one-page maximum. Its purpose is to give busy decision-makers a high-level understanding of your strategy without having to read the full document. Think of it as the highlights reel. Here is what to include. Start with the client name and a brief project overview. For example, this strategy document outlines a comprehensive marketing automation system for ABC Coaching designed to support their growth objectives over the next six months. Then summarize the primary business goal in one sentence. For example, the primary goal is to increase monthly revenue from $20,000 to $35,000 by acquiring 50 additional clients. Next, provide a 2-3 sentence summary of your core strategy approach. For example, we will achieve this through a multi-channel lead generation campaign combining Facebook ads and organic content, optimized landing pages with high converting offers, and automated email nurture sequences that guide prospects from awareness to purchase This approach is designed to generate 125 qualified leads per month and convert them at a 15 rate Include the expected outcomes and timeline For example expected outcomes include 125 qualified leads per month a 15 lead conversion rate and in monthly revenue within 6 months Finally, provide a high-level budget overview. For example, total investment over 6 months, $27,782, including setup, monthly management, platform costs, and ad spend. Expected ROI, $75,000 in new revenue, a 2.7x return on investment. The executive summary should be written in clear, confident language. Avoid jargon. Write like you are explaining the plan to a smart business owner who does not know marketing terminology. Now let's move to section 2, Business Overview and Goals. The purpose of this section is to demonstrate that you understand your client's business deeply. This builds credibility and trust. Start with company background. Include basic information like what the business does, who they serve, how long they have been in business, their market position, and their competitive landscape. You do not need to write a novel here, just a few paragraphs that show you understand their business. For example, ABC Coaching is a 3-year-old online fitness coaching business founded by Jane Smith, a certified personal trainer with over 10 years of experience. The business serves busy professionals aged 30 to 45 who want to get fit but do not have time for traditional gym memberships. ABC Coaching offers personalized home workout programs, nutrition guidance, and accountability coaching delivered entirely online. Next, list their products and services with brief descriptions and pricing. This comes from your work in Lesson 2.2. For example, ABC Coaching offers three core programs, the Starter Program at $99 per month, the Premium Program at $199 per month, and the Elite Program at $399 per month. Then describe their current business situation. What is working? What is not? What challenges are they facing? For example, ABC Coaching currently has 35 active clients generating approximately $20,000 in monthly revenue. The business has grown primarily through referrals and organic social media, but growth has plateaued over the past six months. The primary challenge is generating a consistent flow of qualified leads without relying solely on referrals. Now present their primary business goals using the SMART framework from Lesson 2.5. For example, primary goal increase monthly revenue from $20,000 to $35,000 within six months. This requires acquiring 50 additional clients at an average program value of $300 per month. Finally, define success criteria. What does winning look like? For example, success will be defined as achieving $35,000 in monthly recurring revenue, maintaining a client retention rate above 85%, and generating a positive ROI on marketing spend of at least 2, 1 within 6 months. This section is typically 2 to 3 pages and sets the context for everything that follows. Section 3 is Target Audience and Customer Personas. This is where you bring your personas from Lesson 2.4 into the Strategy document. This section should be highly visual and engaging. Start with a brief target audience overview. Describe the demographics and psychographics of the people you are marketing to. For example, ADC Coaching's target audience consists of busy professionals aged 30 to 45, primarily in white-collar occupations, with household incomes above $75,000. They value efficiency, results, and flexibility. They are willing to invest in their health but need solutions that fit into their demanding schedules. Then present your customer personas. Use visual persona cards with photos or avatars. Each persona should take up half a page to a full page and include all the elements you created in Lesson 2.4. Name, age, occupation, photo, background, goals, challenges, values, preferred channels, buying behavior, and the representative quote. For example, you might have Persona 1, Busy Professional Sarah, Persona 2, New Mom Emily, and Persona 3, Career-Focused David. Each persona should feel like a real person your client can visualize and empathize with. Include a brief note about how these personas will guide your market. For example, every landing page, email, and ad will be crafted specifically for one of these personas, ensuring messaging is relevant and resonates with the target audience. This section is typically 3-4 pages and is one of the most visually engaging parts of the document. Section 4 is Current State Analysis. This section documents where the client is starting from so you can measure progress later. Start by describing their current marketing efforts. What are they doing now, what is working and what is not? For example, ABC Coaching currently posts on Instagram three times per week, sends occasional email newsletters to a list of 850 subscribers, and relies primarily on word-of-mouth referrals. Instagram engagement is moderate but has not translated into consistent leads. Email open rates average 18% below industry benchmarks. List their existing tools and platforms. For example, current tools include Instagram for social media, MailChimp for email marketing, and Acuity Scheduling for appointment booking. There is no CRM or marketing automation platform currently in use. Present their current performance metrics as a baseline. Use actual data if available or estimates based on your conversations with the client. For example, Current baseline metrics, Website traffic approximately 500 visitors per month, Email list of 850 subscribers, Average of 15 new leads per month, 5 new clients per month, $20,000 in monthly revenue. Identify gaps and opportunities. What is missing? Where are the the biggest opportunities for improvement. For example, key gaps include no dedicated lead generation system, no landing pages optimized for conversion, no automated nurture sequences, limited tracking and analytics, and inconsistent follow-up with leads. Opportunities include implementing Facebook advertising to scale lead generation, creating high converting landing pages, and building automated nurture sequences to improve conversion rates. This section is typically 2 to 3 pages and establishes the before picture that will make your results look even more impressive. Section 5 is Marketing Automation Strategy, and this is the heart of your document. This is where you present your recommended approach in detail. Start with an overall strategy summary. Give the big picture in a few paragraphs. For example, our strategy is to build a scalable, automated lead generation and nurture system that consistently attracts qualified prospects, educates them about ABC Coaching's unique approach, and converts them into paying clients. We will use a combination of paid advertising for rapid lead acquisition, optimized landing pages for high conversion rates, and automated email sequences for nurture and conversion. Then break down your channel strategy. Explain which marketing channels you will use and why. For example, primary channel, Facebook and Instagram ads. We will use paid social advertising to reach busy professionals in our target demographic with compelling offers and lead magnets. Secondary channel, email marketing. We will build automated email sequences that nurture leads, provide value and guide prospects toward booking a consultation. Supporting channel, organic social media. We will maintain a consistent presence on Instagram to build brand awareness and credibility. Describe the campaign types you will implement. For example, Campaign 1. Lead Generation Campaign. Facebook ads driving traffic to a landing page offering a free 7-day home workout challenge. Campaign 2. Nurture Sequence. 5 email automated sequence educating leads about the benefits of ABC Coaching's approach and inviting them to book a free consultation. Campaign 3. Consultation Conversion Campaign Reminder sequences and follow-up communications to convert consultation bookings into paying clients. Include your funnel design. This is where you present a visual funnel diagram showing the stages from awareness to purchase, the number of people at each stage, and the conversion rates. This visual is incredibly powerful because it shows the client exactly how leads will flow through the system. Explain your automation workflows. What will be automated and how will automation support the goals? For example, key automations include new lead welcome sequence triggered immediately after lead magnet download, consultation reminder sequence triggered when someone books a consultation, and re-engagement sequence for leads who do not book a consultation within 7 days. Finally, outline your content strategy. What content needs to be created and what are the messaging themes? For example, content requirements include 3 landing pages, lead magnet, consultation booking, thank you, 2 email sequences, nurture and consultation reminders, 5 social media ad creatives, and 1 lead magnet, 7-day home workout challenge guide. This section is typically 4 to 6 pages and is where you really showcase your strategic thinking. Section 6 is implementation plan and timeline. This breaks the project down into actionable phases with specific tasks, deliverables, and deadlines. Start by outlining your project phases A typical marketing automation project has three to four phases For example Phase 1 Setup and Configuration Weeks 1 Finest 2 Setup Go High Level Account Configure Domain and Email Install Tracking Pixels and Prepare All Technical Infrastructure Phase 2, Asset Creation, Weeks 3, 4, Create Landing Pages, Write Email Sequences, Design Lead Magnet, and Develop Ad Creatives. Phase 3 Campaign Launch, Weeks 5-6 Launch Facebook Ad Campaign, Activate Email Automations, and Begin Driving Traffic Phase 4 Monitor and Optimize Ongoing Collect performance data, conduct A-B testing, and optimize campaigns for improved results. For each phase, provide a detailed task list with specific deliverables and who is responsible. Be clear about what you will do versus what the client needs to provide. For example, Phase 1 Tasks. Marketing Automation Specialist will set up Go High Level account, configure domain DNS settings, and install Facebook Pixel. Client will provide brand assets, logo, colors, fonts, access to existing platforms, and product information. Include dependencies, what needs to happen before what. For example, Note Phase 2 cannot begin until client provides brand assets and product information. Phase 3 cannot begin until all landing pages and email sequences are approved by client. Identify key milestones and approval gates. For example, Milestone 1, Technical Setup Complete by End of Week 2. Milestone 2, All Assets Created and Submitted for Client Approval by End of Week 4. A Gantt chart or horizontal timeline makes it easy to see the project flow at a glance. This section is typically 2 to 3 pages and gives everyone a clear roadmap for execution. Section 7 is Budget and Resources, and this is where transparency is key. Clients appreciate when you are upfront about costs rather than surprising them later. Break your budget down into clear categories. Start with your service fees. For example, marketing automation services, $2,500 one-time setup fee for strategy, configuration, and initial asset creation, $1,500 per month for ongoing management, optimization, and reporting. List tool and platform costs. For example, Go High Level Platform, $297 per month. This includes CRM, email marketing, landing page builder, automation workflows, and reporting. Canva Pro for Design, $12.95 per month. Include advertising budget. For example, recommended advertising budget, $2,000 per month for Facebook and Instagram ads. This budget is designed to generate approximately 125 leads per month at a cost per lead of $16. Include any additional costs. For example, stock photos for landing pages, approximately $100 one time. Professional headshot for client if needed. Client responsibility. Provide a total investment summary. Break it down into one-time costs, monthly recurring costs, and total 6-month investment. Always include a note about expected ROI. For example, total 6-month investment, $27,782. Expected return $75,000 in new revenue representing a 2.7x return on investment Being transparent about costs, builds trust, and eliminates surprises, clients will respect your honesty and professionalism. This section is typically 2 pages. Section 8 is Success Metrics and KPIs This section defines exactly how success will be measured and comes directly from your work in Lesson 2.5. Start by listing your primary KPIs. These are the metrics that matter most. For example, primary KPIs. Monthly Qualified Leads, Target 125, Landing Page Conversion Rate, Target 12%, Lead to Customer Conversion Rate, Target 15%, Monthly Revenue, Target $35,000. List your secondary KPIs. These are supporting metrics. For example, secondary KPIs, email open rate, target above 25%, email click-through rate, target above 4%, cost per lead, target below $50, customer acquisition cost, target below $300. For each KPI, show the current baseline, the 3-month target, and the 6-month target. Use a table like the one in Infographic Hash 15 to make this clear and visual. Explain your reporting cadence. How often will you share performance data? For example, reporting cadence, weekly dashboard updates shared via email, monthly detailed performance reports with analysis and recommendations, Quarterly Strategy Review Meetings to assess progress and adjust approach. Include a dashboard preview or mock-up. Show the client what the reporting will look like. You can create a simple mock-up in Google Sheets, Canva, or even a screenshot from GoHighLevel. This makes the abstract concept of reporting feel real and tangible. This section is typically 2 to 3 pages and gives the client confidence that you will be tracking and reporting on what matters. Section 9 is Next Steps and Call to Action. This is where you tell the client exactly what happens next. Provide a decision timeline. For example, please review this strategy document by Friday, February 7. We recommend starting the project by Monday, February 17 to allow time for setup before the March campaign launch. Outline what happens next. For example, step 1, schedule a strategy review call to discuss this document and answer any questions. Step 2, upon approval, we will send a service agreement and invoice for the setup fee. Step 3, once the agreement is signed and payment is received, we will schedule a kickoff meeting and begin phase 1. List what the client needs to provide. For example, I am available via email at yournameatemail.com or by phone at 555-123-4567. I am happy to schedule a call to discuss any aspect of this strategy and with a clear call to action. For example, ready to get started? Let's schedule a 30-minute strategy review call. You can book directly on my calendar here. I look forward to working together to achieve your growth goals. This section is typically one page and provides clear direction for moving forward. Now let's talk about how to make your strategy document look professional. Content is king, but presentation matters too. First, use your client's brand colors and logo throughout the document. This makes the document feel custom and shows attention to detail. Include their logo on the cover page and in the header or footer of every page. Use consistent professional fonts. Stick to two fonts maximum, one for headers and one for body text. Good choices include sans-serif fonts like Arial, Helvetica, or Calibri for clean modern documents, or serif fonts like Georgia or Times New Roman for more traditional documents. Include a table of contents if your document is longer than 10 pages. This makes navigation easy and shows organization. Use headers and sub-headers to break up text. No one wants to read walls of text. Break your content into sections with clear headers and use sub-headers within sections to further organize information. Incorporate visual elements throughout. Include persona cards with photos, funnel diagrams, timeline graphics, budget tables, and KPI dashboards. Visuals make the document more engaging and easier to understand. Leave white space. Do not cram too much information onto each page. White space makes documents easier to read and more visually appealing. Include page numbers. This helps with navigation and makes the document feel polished. Proofread multiple times. Typos and errors undermine your credibility. Read the document out loud. Use spellcheck. And consider having someone else review it before you send it to the client. Export as a clean PDF. PDFs are professional, preserve formatting, and can be viewed on any device. If possible, create a PDF with a clickable table of contents for easy navigation. What tools should you use to create strategy documents? You have several options and the best choice depends on your design skills and budget. Google Docs is free, collaborative, and easy to share. It is great for text-heavy documents and allows real-time collaboration with clients. The downside is limited design capabilities compared to other tools. Microsoft Word is professional with robust formatting options. It is the industry standard for business documents. The downside is it requires a license and is less collaborative than Google Docs. Canva is excellent for creating visually stunning documents. It offers beautiful templates and is easy to use even if you are not a designer. The downside is documents can look templated if you do not customize them enough. Google Slides or PowerPoint can be used for more visual, presentation-style strategy documents. They offer flexibility and strong visual design options. The downside is it can be harder to make them look like traditional documents rather than slide decks My recommendation for beginners start with Google Docs for the structure and content then use Canva to create visual elements like persona cards funnel diagrams and timelines Export everything as PDF before sending to clients As you gain experience and build your business, you might invest in more advanced tools or hire a designer to create custom templates for you. Let's talk about the most common mistakes people make when creating strategy documents so you can avoid them. Mistake number one, making the document too long or too complicated. Clients are busy. They do not want to read a 50-page dissertation. Keep your document focused and concise. 15 to 25 pages is the sweet spot for most engagements. Mistake number two, using too much jargon. Remember, you are writing for business owners, not other marketing automation specialists. Avoid terms like CTR, 20FU, DOFU, or attribution modeling unless you explain them in plain English. Write the way you would talk to a smart friend who does not work in marketing. Mistake number three, having no visual elements. A document that is all text is boring and hard to read. Include diagrams, charts, persona cards, and timelines to break up the text and make information easier to digest. Mistake number four. Vague goals and metrics. Saying we will improve your marketing is not good enough. Be specific. We will increase monthly leads from 50 to 125 within six months, resulting in $35,000 in monthly revenue. Mistake number five. No clear next steps. Do not leave the client wondering what happens after they read the document. Always end with specific next steps and a clear call to action. Mistake number six. Forgetting the client's budget reality. It is easy to recommend an elaborate strategy that costs $50,000 when the client only has $10,000 to spend. Be realistic about what can be achieved within their budget or present tiered options. Mistake number seven. Not customizing for each client. Never send a generic template with just the client name swapped out. Clients can tell every strategy document should feel custom and specific to that client's unique situation. Now it is time for you to create your first strategy document. This is where everything from module two comes together. Here is your exercise. First, choose a business. This can be a real business you are working with, a business you want to approach, or a completely hypothetical business you create for practice. Second, gather all the information you need from your previous lessons. Pull together your discovery notes from Lesson 2.1, your customer journey map from Lesson 2.3, your personas from Lesson 2.4, and your goals and KPIs from Lesson 2.5. Third, create an outline with all nine sections, executive summary, business overview and goals, target audience and personas, current state analysis, marketing automation strategy, implementation plan and timeline, budget and resources, success metrics and KPIs, and next steps. Fourth, fill in at least three sections completely. I recommend starting with target audience and personas, success metrics and KPIs, and next steps because those are the most straightforward. Then tackle business overview and goals and marketing automation strategy. Fifth, design at least two visual elements. Create a persona card with a photo and all the persona details and create either a funnel diagram or a KPI tracking table. Use Canva, Google Slides, or even draw it by hand and scan it. The point is to practice creating visual elements. Sixth, format everything as a professional-looking PDF. Pay attention to fonts, spacing, headers, and overall layout as a bonus challenge create a full 15-20 page strategy document for a real business and present it to them. If you do not have a real client yet, practice presenting your document to a friend or family member and ask for feedback on clarity and persuasiveness. This exercise is going to take time. You might spend several hours or even a few days completing it. That is normal. Creating your first strategy document is challenging, but every one you create after this will be faster and easier. You are building a skill that will serve you for your entire career. Let's recap the key takeaways from this lesson. A marketing automation strategy document is your blueprint. It aligns everyone on goals, strategy, and execution before work begins. It protects you from scope creep and demonstrates your professionalism. Your document should include nine core sections, executive summary, business overview and goals, target audience and personas, current state analysis, marketing automation strategy, implementation plan and timeline, budget and resources, success metrics and KPIs, and next steps. Make your document visual. Include persona cards, funnel diagrams, timelines, budget tables, and KPI tracking tables. Visuals make information easier to understand and more engaging. Be transparent about costs and realistic about timelines. Clients appreciate honesty. Surprises erode trust. Tie everything back to measurable business goals and KPIs. Every recommendation you make should connect to a specific objective and success metric. Write for your client, not for other marketers. Use clear, simple language. Avoid jargon. Explain technical concepts in plain English Always end with clear next steps and a call to action Make it easy for the client to say yes and move forward This document is proof of your strategic thinking and professionalism It is what separates you from technicians and positions you as a true marketing automation specialist Congratulations! You have just completed Module 2, Understanding Business and Client Requirements This is a huge milestone Let's take a moment to reflect on everything you have learned. You now know how to conduct professional client discovery calls that uncover the real needs, goals, and challenges of any business. You can identify and document products, services, and offers in a way that reveals opportunities for marketing automation. You understand how to map complete customer journeys from awareness to advocacy, identifying every touchpoint where marketing automation can add value. You can create detailed research-backed customer personas that bring your target audience to life and guide all your marketing decisions. You know how to define business goals, translate them into smart objectives, and identify the key performance indicators that matter most. And you can synthesize all of this information into a comprehensive professional marketing automation strategy document that gets client buy-in and sets the foundation for success. These are not basic skills. These are the strategic, high-level skills that command premium rates and long-term client relationships. Many people who call themselves marketing automation specialists never learn these skills. They jump straight to tactics without understanding strategy. You are different. You now have the foundation to approach any client engagement with confidence and professionalism. So what is next? In Module 3, we are going to shift from strategy to tactics. You are going to learn about the two primary categories of marketing campaigns, inbound and outbound. You will understand what inbound marketing is, including content marketing, SEO, and social media. You will learn what outbound marketing is, including ads, cold email, and SMS. You will discover when to use each approach and how to combine inbound and outbound strategies into integrated campaigns that generate qualified leads. and you will learn specific lead generation tactics for each campaign type along with a campaign planning and execution checklist you can use for every campaign you build. Module 3 is where marketing automation starts to get really exciting because you will learn how to actually attract and capture leads at scale. I will see you there. visualDescriptions.description:
The image presents a slide from a presentation on creating a marketing automation strategy document, titled "Creating a Marketing Automation Strategy Document" in large black text. The title is accompanied by the subtitle "Your Blueprint for Client Success" in smaller gray text.

**Key Elements:**

* **Title and Subtitle:**
	+ Title: "Creating a Marketing Automation Strategy Document"
	+ Subtitle: "Your Blueprint for Client Success"
* **Libre Academy Logo:**
	+ Located in the top-left corner
	+ Features the text "Libre Academy" in green, with "MODULE 2 • LESSON 2.6" written below it
* **LIBRE Logo:**
	+ Positioned in the top-right corner
	+ Displays the word "LIBRE" in green
* **Central Graphic:**
	+ A white circle with a green document icon at its center
	+ Three tabs extending from the circle:
		- **Structure**
		- **Action Plan**
		- **Clear Goals**
* **Bottom Section:**
	+ Three tabs:
		- **Strategy Blueprint**
		- **Implementation Plan**
		- **Metrics & KPIs**

The background of the slide features a white-to-light-blue gradient, providing a clean and professional visual setting for the content. Overall, the image effectively conveys the importance of a well-structured marketing automation strategy document for achieving client success.

The image displays a slide from a presentation on marketing automation strategy. 

**Title:** "Marketing Automation Strategy"

**Section:** Section 5

**Subtitle:** "The heart of the document - your recommended strategic approach."

**Core Strategy Components:**

* Overall Strategy Summary
* Channel Strategy
* Campaign Types
* Funnel Design
* Automation Workflows
* Content Strategy

**Strategy Summary Example:** A text box on the right side of the slide provides an example of a strategy summary, which includes an overview of the marketing automation strategy.

**Strategy Alignment Tip:** A yellow box at the bottom of the slide offers a tip for aligning strategy, which reads: "Review the 'Why'. How does your target align to your goals, resources, and tech to buy to help?" 

The slide features a white background with black text and green accents, and the Libre logo is displayed in the top-right corner.

The image presents a slide from a presentation titled "Implementation Plan & Timeline," which outlines the purpose and key components of a project plan. The title is prominently displayed at the top, accompanied by a green bar and the word "LIBRE" in green text.

**Purpose:**
The purpose of the implementation plan and timeline is to show exactly when the HOW revolution will happen and when the deliverables will be completed to build confidence.

**Key Components:**
The key components to include in the plan are:

* Detailed tasks organized by project phase
* Specific deliverables and responsible owners (who vs. client)
* Estimated completion dates for each major task
* Dependencies that must be in place
* Approval milestones and checkpoints

**Standard Project Phases:**
The standard project phases are listed on the right side of the image, including:

* **Phase 1: Sales & Scoping** (Weeks 1-2)
* **Phase 2: Asset Creation** (Weeks 2-4)
* **Phase 3: Campaign Launch** (Weeks 3-4)
* **Phase 4: Optimization** (Weeks 5-8)

**Client Expectation:**
The client expectation is to receive frequent status updates to manage expectations and ensure a smooth campaign. 

Overall, the image provides a clear and concise overview of the implementation plan and timeline, highlighting the key components and standard project phases involved in the project.

The image presents a slide from a presentation, titled "Budget & Resources," which appears to be part of a larger educational or professional development program.

**Title and Section**
The title "Budget & Resources" is prominently displayed at the top-left corner of the slide, accompanied by a green bar with the words "SECTION 7" in white text. In the top-right corner, the word "LIBRE" appears in green text.

**Purpose**
The purpose of this section is to provide a transparent breakdown of all costs and requirements, ensuring that clients understand the full investment required for success.

**Key Components**
The slide outlines several key components to include in a budget:

* **Service Fees**: Professional fees, retainer structure, and payment terms
* **Tool & Platform Costs**: Subscriptions for Google, email, software, etc.
* **Advertising Budget**: Recommended monthly spend per channel
* **Additional Costs**: Budget for costs such as travel, outside vendors, or premium plugins
* **Total Investment Summary**: A clear distinction between one-time setup and recurring costs

**Transparency Note**
A transparency note is provided, stating that the company completely transparent about its costs. The note includes examples of library structures, MS 365, and Gmail.

**Building Client Trust**
A yellow box at the bottom of the slide emphasizes the importance of building client trust by being transparent about costs, rather than hiding them.

Overall, this slide provides a clear and concise overview of the key components to consider when creating a budget and ensuring transparency with clients.

The image displays a slide from a presentation on "Success Metrics & KPIs." The slide is divided into two columns.

**Left Column:**

* **Title:** Success Metrics & KPIs
* **Purpose:** Define exactly how success will be measured, linking marketing activities directly to measurable business outcomes.
* **Key Performance Indicators (KPI) Components (5):**
	1. **Primary KPIs:** The most important metrics tied directly to business objectives (e.g., Revenue).
	2. **Secondary KPIs:** Supporting metrics that reveal Open Rates, CTR, and Landing Page Performance.
	3. **Targets to Each KPI:** Clear definition of current baseline, target goals, and what needs to be achieved.
	4. **Reporting Cadence:** Schedule for weekly dashboard reviews, monthly reports, and strategy meetings.
	5. **Dashboard Preview:** A visual sample of how metrics will be tracked and displayed.

**Right Column:**

* **KPI Definition Structure:**
	+ Primary KPI: Simplified Leads
		- Business Objective: X amount of leads
		- Target: Month/Quarter/year target for KPI
	+ Secondary KPIs: Conversion Rate, Cost per Lead, % Lead Increase
* **Key Message:** A yellow box with a key icon contains the text: 
	+ "This is how we prove the measurement is working." 
	+ "When metrics are tracked and aligned to business objectives, success becomes obvious." 

The Libre logo appears in the top-right corner.

The image displays a presentation slide focused on the next steps and call to action for a project.

**Title:** "Next Steps & Call to Action"

**Purpose:** Define a clear, unambiguous path forward to get started with the project implementation.

**Key Components:**
1. Decision Timeline: Review by \[Date], Start by \[Date]
2. What Happens Next: Strategy review, contract signing, kickoff meeting
3. Client Requirements: Brand assets, platform access, content, testimonials
4. Contact Information: Email, phone, availability
5. Call to Action: "Ready to get started? Let's schedule a call."

**Sample Next Steps Sequence:**
- Review: Please review the strategy by Friday
- Approve: Sign the proposal sent via email
- Launch: Schedule our Kickoff Call

**Tone of Voice:** Keep this section short, professional, and action-oriented. You are guiding the client to the "next" element they need to do or learn.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to best practices for designing strategy documents, titled "Strategy Document Design Best Practices" in bold black text at the top. The title is accompanied by a green logo featuring the word "LIBRE" in the upper-right corner.

The infographic is divided into three columns, each with a green header and a list of bullet points providing detailed information on the following topics:

**Design Tips**

* Use client's brand colors and logos throughout
* Include table of contents for documents over 5 pages
* Use headers and subheaders for easy navigation
* Break up long text blocks with visuals and charts
* Use contrast, professional fonts (e.g., Arial, Helvetica)
* Include page numbers in the footer
* Leave ample white space to improve readability

**Visual Elements**

* Precise charts with photos or sketches
* Funnel diagrams showing the customer journey
* Flowcharts for implementation phases
* Clear budget tables and cost breakdowns
* Wireframe mockups to show reporting
* Before/After comparisons or state diagrams

**Professional Polish**

* Proofread multiple times for typos and grammar
* Double-check numbers and calculations
* Know consistent formatting across all sections
* Export as a clean, high-resolution PDF
* Add disclaimers in the Table of Contents
* Include full contact information on the last page

At the bottom of the image, a yellow oval contains the text "Target length: Typically 10-20 pages for a comprehensive strategy document." The background of the image is white, providing a clean and professional backdrop for the information presented. Overall, the infographic provides a clear and concise guide to designing effective strategy documents.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to tools for creating strategy documents, featuring four prominent options: Google Docs, Microsoft Word, Canva, and Slides/PPT. Each tool is accompanied by its logo, pros, cons, and best use cases.

* **Google Docs**
	+ Logo: Blue square with a white "D"
	+ Pros:
		- Real-time collaboration
		- Easy to share files
	+ Cons:
		- Limited design capabilities
		- Not ideal for "final" documents
	+ Best for: Simple, text-heavy documents & collaboration
* **Microsoft Word**
	+ Logo: Black square with a white "W"
	+ Pros:
		- Robust formatting
		- Professionally standard
	+ Cons:
		- Requires some learning curve
		- Limited collaboration
	+ Best for: Detailed, polished corporate documents
* **Canva**
	+ Logo: Green square with a white circle
	+ Pros:
		- Beautiful templates
		- Easy drag-and-drop
		- Editing on-the-go
	+ Cons:
		- Limited advanced features
		- Not ideal for detailed writing
	+ Best for: Highly visual, client-facing presentations
* **Slides/PPT**
	+ Logo: Yellow square with a white presentation icon
	+ Pros:
		- Slide layout
		- Easy to present
	+ Cons:
		- Not ideal for in-depth analysis
		- Limited to sequential (linear) presentation
	+ Best for: Visual strategy decks & presentation

The image also includes a green banner at the bottom with a recommendation to start with Google Docs or Canva templates for first-time projects and then graduate to more advanced tools like InDesign or specialized software as you gain experience. Overall, the image provides a clear and concise comparison of the four tools, helping users choose the best option for their strategy document needs.

The image presents a concise and informative guide to common strategy document mistakes and their corresponding solutions. The title, **Common Strategy Document Mistakes**, is prominently displayed at the top, accompanied by the logo for LIBRE in the upper-right corner.

**Mistakes and Solutions:**

The image features a table with two columns, listing seven common mistakes on the left and their solutions on the right. The mistakes are presented in red text, while the solutions are in green. The table is organized as follows:

* **THE MISTAKE**
	+ Making It Too Long or Too Complicated
	+ Using Too Much Jargon
	+ No Visual Elements (Wall of Text)
	+ Vague Goals and Metrics
	+ No Clear Next Steps
	+ Ignoring Budget Reality
	+ Not Customizing for Each Client
* **THE SOLUTION**
	+ Keep it focused — 10-15 pages maximum
	+ Write for your client, not other marketers
	+ Include diagrams, charts, and personas cards
	+ Be specific with numbers, targets, and deadlines
	+ Always end with specific calls to action
	+ Be realistic and transparent about all costs
	+ Never use a generic template — personalize everything

**Key Takeaways:**

The image effectively highlights the importance of avoiding common pitfalls when creating strategy documents. By following the solutions provided, individuals can create clear, concise, and effective documents that cater to their clients' needs. The use of visual elements, such as diagrams and charts, can help to break up the text and make the document more engaging. Additionally, being specific with goals and metrics, and providing clear next steps, can help to ensure that the document is actionable and effective.

Overall, the image provides a valuable resource for individuals looking to improve their strategy document creation skills and avoid common mistakes.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to creating a strategy document, titled **"Practice Exercise: Create Your First Strategy Document."** The title is prominently displayed at the top of the page.

**Sectional Breakdown**

The main content is organized into six sections, each with a green checkmark and a brief description:

1. **Choose a Business**: Select a real or hypothetical business to serve as a case study.
2. **Gather Information**: Collect data from previous modules, lessons, and case studies.
3. **Create Outline**: Structure the document with key sections.
4. **Fill Key Sections**: Outline content for at least 1,000 words across sections such as:
	* Executive summary
	* Situation analysis
	* Strategic options and recommendations
	* Implementation plan
	* Metrics and KPIs
5. **Design Visuals**: Create 2-3 visuals to support text, including:
	* Finalized diagrams or infographics
6. **Format & Export**: Apply branding, resize and export the document, and share it as a PDF or link.

**Additional Features**

To the right of the main content, two highlighted sections provide supplementary information:

* **Bonus Challenge**: A challenge to create a 10-20-page strategy document for a real business, with a suggested PDF template for practice purposes.
* **What You'll Learn**: A summary of the skills and knowledge gained through this exercise, including:
	+ Synthesizing toward strategy
	+ Professional document creation
	* Strategic planning frameworks
	+ Clear, easy document design

**Visual Elements**

The image features a **LIBRE** logo in the top-right corner and a **LESSON 2.4 EXERCISE** banner in the top-left corner. The background of the image transitions from white to a light green gradient.

The image presents a concise summary of the key takeaways from Lesson 2.6, titled "Key Takeaways from Lesson 2.6" in bold green text at the top left corner. The title is accompanied by the logo for "LIBRE" in green and yellow to its right.

Below the title, a white box with rounded corners contains seven bullet points, each preceded by a green square. The bullet points outline essential elements of a strategy document:

*   **The Blueprint**: A strategy document serves as a roadmap, aligning the client and team before work begins.
*   **Core Structure**: Include essential sections such as Executive Summary, Business Overview, Personas, Current State, Strategy, Timeline, Budget, KPIs, and Next Steps.
*   **Visual Impact**: Make it engaging with visual elements like persona cards, funnel diagrams, timeline graphics, and KPI tables.
*   **Transparency**: Be completely transparent about costs/budget and set realistic expectations for timelines to build trust.
*   **Data-Driven**: Tie every strategy decision back to measurable business goals and specific KPIs established in discovery.
*   **Client-Centric**: Write for your client, not other marketers—avoid jargon and focus on business outcomes.
*   **Clear Path Forward**: Always end the document with specific next steps and a clear call to action.
*   **Professionalism**: This document serves as a tangible demonstration of your professionalism and strategic thinking capabilities.

The background of the image features a light green color, providing a clean and visually appealing contrast to the white box and green accents. Overall, the image effectively summarizes the key takeaways from Lesson 2.6, providing a clear and concise overview of the essential elements of a strategy document.

The background of the slide is white, with black text and green accents.

## Step 1: Identify the Purpose
The purpose of defining a target audience and creating **customer personas** is to consolidate demographic data and psychographic insights to **define exactly who you're marketing to**. This helps tailor marketing efforts more effectively.

## Step 2: Key Components to Include
The key components to include in a customer persona are:
- **Primary Audience**: Demographics (e.g., age, gender) and Psychographics (e.g., interests, values).
- **2-4 Detailed Customer Personas**: 
  - **Persona Details**: Name, Age, Occupation, Photo
  - **Specific goals, aspirations, challenges, and pain points**
  - **Preferred channels and buying behavior**
- **A representative quote** that captures their mindset.

## Step 3: Why This Matters
Understanding your target audience and creating detailed personas matters because every **target segment, market, and landing page** should be **designed specifically** for these people. **Vague targets lead to ineffective marketing.**

## Step 4: Persona Card Layout
A persona card layout typically includes:
- A **profile picture or avatar** to humanize the persona.
- **Demographic and psychographic details** such as age, occupation, and goals.
- A **quote** that encapsulates their mindset.

## Step 5: Presentation Tip
When presenting personas, use **persona cards with real photos**, not just text to **make the target real** to the audience.

## Conclusion
The slide emphasizes the importance of well-defined **customer personas** in marketing. By **consolidating demographic and psychographic data**, businesses can create targeted and effective marketing strategies. **Detailed personas** help ensure that marketing efforts resonate with the intended audience.
